event: Pre-ceramic Archaic hunter-gatherer peoples, related to mainland South American groups, settle on Aruba and live by fishing and gathering.
The Caquetio people, an Arawak-speaking group migrating from the Venezuelan mainland, settle Aruba and establish ceramic-using farming villages.
Spanish explorer Alonso de Ojeda, accompanied by Amerigo Vespucci, reaches Aruba and claims it for the Spanish Crown.
Spain declares Aruba, Bonaire, and Curaçao 'islas inútiles' (useless islands) and deports many indigenous Caquetio to work in mines on Hispaniola.
Spain begins using Aruba as a cattle-ranching outpost, reintroducing settlers and livestock to the island.
The Dutch West India Company seizes Aruba from Spain during the Eighty Years' War, bringing the island under Dutch control.
The Peace of Westphalia confirms Dutch possession of Aruba as part of the colonial holdings of the Netherlands.
Aruba's first European settlement and Catholic mission activity expand, while the island remains a Dutch West India Company horse-and-cattle ranch.
Control of Aruba passes from the dissolved Dutch West India Company to the Batavian Republic's colonial administration.
British forces briefly occupy Aruba during the Napoleonic Wars amid conflict with the Dutch.
Britain again occupies Aruba during the Napoleonic Wars, holding the island until the Dutch regain control.
Aruba is formally restored to Dutch rule following the Napoleonic Wars under the Anglo-Dutch Treaty arrangements.
Gold is discovered at Rooi Fluit, sparking a gold rush that becomes a key part of Aruba's 19th-century economy.
Aloe cultivation is introduced to Aruba, and the island grows into one of the world's leading exporters of aloe.
The Netherlands abolishes slavery in its Caribbean colonies, freeing the enslaved population of Aruba.
The Aruba Island Goldmining Company begins large-scale gold operations, and a smelting works is later built at Bushiribana.
Phosphate mining at Seroe Colorado adds to Aruba's mineral exports during the late 19th century.
Declining yields and disruptions of World War I bring Aruba's gold-mining era to an end.
The Lago Oil and Transport Company establishes facilities at San Nicolas, beginning Aruba's transformation into an oil-refining center.
Standard Oil of New Jersey acquires Lago and builds a massive oil refinery at San Nicolas that becomes one of the world's largest.
A German U-boat attacks Aruba's refinery and shipping during World War II, briefly shelling the island in the Battle of the Caribbean.
Aruban leader Henny Eman presents the first formal proposal for Aruba's separation (Status Aparte) from the rest of the Netherlands Antilles.
Aruban political movements intensify their campaign for autonomy and separation from Curaçao-dominated colonial governance.
The Charter for the Kingdom of the Netherlands grants the Netherlands Antilles, including Aruba, internal self-government within the Kingdom.
Betico Croes founds the political movement that drives Aruba's push for separate status and eventual independence.
Aruba adopts its own flag and the anthem 'Aruba Dushi Tera,' asserting a distinct national identity.
A referendum on Aruba shows overwhelming popular support for self-determination and separation from the Netherlands Antilles.
A Round Table Conference in The Hague agrees that Aruba will attain separate status (Status Aparte) within the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
The Lago oil refinery closes, devastating Aruba's economy and prompting a major shift toward tourism.
Aruba attains Status Aparte on January 1, becoming a separate autonomous country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
Aruba holds its first elections as an autonomous country, with Henny Eman becoming its first Prime Minister.
Betico Croes, the father of Aruban autonomy, dies after a car accident and is later honored as a national hero.
Aruba and the Netherlands agree to indefinitely postpone the originally planned 1996 full independence, keeping Aruba within the Kingdom.
The Coastal Aruba refinery reopens, restarting oil processing as tourism continues its rapid growth.
Aruba's tourism sector booms as high-rise resorts along Palm Beach establish the island as a major Caribbean destination.
Aruba's economy becomes overwhelmingly dependent on tourism, with North American visitors driving sustained growth.
The disappearance of American teenager Natalee Holloway draws intense international media attention to Aruba.
The Valero-owned refinery at San Nicolas suspends operations, again straining Aruba's economy amid the global financial crisis.
The dissolution of the Netherlands Antilles reshapes the Kingdom, leaving Aruba as one of its constituent countries alongside Curaçao and Sint Maarten.
Aruba sets a national goal of transitioning to fully renewable energy, investing heavily in wind and solar power.
Aruba expands ties with the European Union as an Overseas Country and Territory while strengthening Caribbean cooperation.
The COVID-19 pandemic shuts down tourism and devastates Aruba's economy, prompting emergency financial support from the Netherlands.
Aruba's tourism begins recovering as travel restrictions ease, restoring the island's primary economic engine.
Aruba commemorates its national heritage and continued autonomy while debating constitutional reform and ties with the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
